description: Rules that apply to ALL generated apps regardless of category.

guidelines.rule: The human user MUST be able to interact with the app. Never create an app that runs entirely on autopilot with no user input.
guidelines.why: Users repeatedly report that generated apps play themselves with no way for the human to participate. This is the #1 most common complaint.
examples: Game: user needs buttons to make decisions
Tool: user needs input fields and action buttons

guidelines.rule: When the goal asks for anything 3D - it literally says '3D' / 'three.js' / 'WebGL' / 'low-poly' / 'voxel' / 'first-person' / 'fly or orbit around', OR it describes an inherently spatial 3D scene (a solar system or planets, a galaxy of stars, a 3D diorama, terrain you move through, an aquarium or reef, a vehicle you drive in a world) - you MUST render it with REAL three.js in WebGL: a THREE.Scene + THREE.PerspectiveCamera + THREE.WebGLRenderer with actual 3D meshes and lights. NEVER substitute a flat 2D <canvas> with getContext('2d') and hand-drawn circles or sprites - that is a failure for a 3D goal even if it looks 'spacey'. Load three.js offline-safely via an importmap (the platform auto-vendors CDN three.js to local files at install, so a standard CDN importmap is correct and works offline): <script type="importmap">{"imports":{"three":"https://unpkg.com/three@0.160.0/build/three.module.js","three/addons/":"https://unpkg.com/three@0.160.0/examples/jsm/"}}</script> then `import * as THREE from 'three'` and `import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js'`. Use OrbitControls for drag-to-orbit and scroll-to-zoom. Keep one full-window canvas, a render loop via requestAnimationFrame, and resize handling.
guidelines.why: Goals whose wording has a strong 2D convention (e.g. 'solar system', 'galaxy') were being built as flat 2D canvas drawings because nothing in the pipeline steered the model to three.js. The exact same path produced real 3D only when the wording (e.g. 'voxel', 'first-person runner') happened to imply 3D. A 3D request must deterministically produce WebGL 3D regardless of phrasing.
examples: GOOD (3D solar system): a THREE.Scene with a SphereGeometry sun (emissive material), planet meshes whose orbit angle advances each frame, a PointLight at the sun, OrbitControls for drag/zoom, a PerspectiveCamera.
BAD: const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); ctx.arc(...) to draw planets as 2D circles - this is NOT 3D and fails the goal.

guidelines.rule: Camera and microphone work in TWO host environments via `window.mChatAI.camera.takePhoto({mirror?, quality?})` and `window.mChatAI.camera.takeVideo({maxSeconds?, mirror?, audio?})`. Inside **mChatAI+** (macOS or iOS) → native AVFoundation capture sheet. Published to the **hub** → a web polyfill is injected at serve time that runs `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia` + an in-page overlay. Either path returns `{dataUrl, width, height}` (photo) or `{dataUrl, durationMs, width, height, mimeType}` (video). Rejects with 'USER_CANCELLED'/'PERMISSION_DENIED'/'NO_CAMERA'/'CAPTURE_FAILED'. Same code, three environments — DO NOT branch on `if (window.mChatAI?.camera?.takePhoto)` anymore; just call it. Best practices: (1) call ONLY after explicit user tap — never on page load; (2) await + catch, show an inline retry message on rejection (never leave a blank screen); (3) for live previews/effects (filters, AR), use `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia` directly and release tracks (`stream.getTracks().forEach(t => t.stop())`) when done.
guidelines.why: Earlier (Phase ROUTE+CAM 2026-06-01) media capture was disabled in mini-apps because the WebView lacked a permission delegate and crashed the host on prompt. Fixed 2026-06-04 (Phase ROUTE+CAM-ENABLE): WKUIDelegate now returns `.prompt`, the standard NSCameraUsageDescription / NSMicrophoneUsageDescription dialogs fire, and entitlements were added to both Debug + DirectDownload builds. Mini-apps can now do Stop Motion / photo booth / DreamSnap-style video / voice memos. The wisdom shifts from 'don't use camera' to 'use camera politely and clean up after yourself'.
examples: GOOD (single call works in mChatAI+ macOS, iOS, AND the hub on any browser): try { const r = await window.mChatAI.camera.takePhoto({mirror:true}); img.src = r.dataUrl; } catch(e) { showError(e.message); }

GOOD (video): try { const r = await window.mChatAI.camera.takeVideo({maxSeconds:10, audio:true}); videoEl.src = r.dataUrl; durationLabel.textContent = (r.durationMs/1000).toFixed(1) + 's'; } catch(e) { if (e.message !== 'USER_CANCELLED') showError(e.message); }

guidelines.rule: Use `window.mChatAI.share.send({title?, text?, url?, dataUrl?})` to invoke the native share sheet on macOS/iOS or `navigator.share` (with copy-link fallback) on the web. Resolves to `{shared: true}` on completion; rejects with 'USER_CANCELLED' / 'NOT_SUPPORTED' / 'SHARE_FAILED: ...'. Pass any combo of fields — `dataUrl` (image/photo) attaches as a File on platforms that support it. Always wire a 'Share' button into game-over screens and content-creation outputs — the bridge handles platform-specific UX for you.
guidelines.why: Hub-published mini-apps go viral when sharing is one tap. `navigator.share` works on iOS Safari and Android Chrome; desktop browsers fall back to clipboard-copy with a toast — handled in the polyfill so you don't write fallback code.
examples: GOOD: shareBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => { try { await window.mChatAI.share.send({title: 'My high score', text: 'I scored ' + score + ' in ' + GAME_NAME, url: location.href}); } catch(e) { if (e.message !== 'USER_CANCELLED') showError(e.message); } });
GOOD (share a captured photo): const photo = await window.mChatAI.camera.takePhoto(); await window.mChatAI.share.send({title: 'Look!', dataUrl: photo.dataUrl, url: location.href});

guidelines.rule: Use `window.mChatAI.location.getCurrent({accuracy?, timeoutSeconds?})` for a one-shot location fix. `accuracy` is 'best' | 'balanced' (default) | 'fast'. Resolves with `{latitude, longitude, accuracyMeters, timestampMs}`. Rejects with 'PERMISSION_DENIED' / 'NO_LOCATION' / 'LOCATION_FAILED: ...'. Native CoreLocation inside mChatAI+; `navigator.geolocation` polyfill on the web. Use for weather cards, treasure hunts, nearby-finder, photo-trip tagging. Never call without explicit user tap on a 'Find my location' button — surprise permission prompts are the #1 complaint.
guidelines.why: Geolocation has been universal across browsers for a decade; the bridge gives ONE call site for both native and web while keeping the permission UX kid-friendly inside mChatAI+.
examples: GOOD: const r = await window.mChatAI.location.getCurrent({accuracy: 'balanced'}); const weather = await fetch('https://api.weather.example/lat=' + r.latitude + '&lon=' + r.longitude).then(r => r.json());

guidelines.rule: Use `window.mChatAI.scanner.scanQR({timeoutSeconds?})` to scan a QR or barcode. Resolves with `{text, format}`. Rejects with 'PERMISSION_DENIED' / 'NO_CAMERA' / 'USER_CANCELLED' / 'SCAN_FAILED: ...'. Native AVCaptureMetadataOutput inside mChatAI+; web polyfill uses getUserMedia + BarcodeDetector API (Chrome/Edge). Safari users without BarcodeDetector see a camera with a hint to install mChatAI+ for instant decoding. Use for multiplayer pairing, classroom treasure hunts, AR scavenger games. The bridge can also decode EAN/UPC/Code128/etc. — useful for grocery/library/inventory mini-apps.
guidelines.why: QR scanning is a high-value UX that's painful to roll yourself with getUserMedia + jsQR. The bridge gives instant native decoding on mChatAI+ and graceful fallback on the web.
examples: GOOD: scanBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => { try { const r = await window.mChatAI.scanner.scanQR(); doSomethingWith(r.text); } catch(e) { if (e.message !== 'USER_CANCELLED') showError(e.message); } });

guidelines.rule: All interactive elements (buttons, inputs, controls) must be large enough to click (minimum 44x44px touch target) and clearly visible.
guidelines.why: Generated apps often have tiny, hard-to-find controls that overlap or get pushed off-screen.
examples: Buttons should have padding: 10px 20px minimum
Don't put buttons inside scrollable areas that can be hidden

guidelines.rule: The app must have a clear visual indication of its current state. The user should always know what is happening and what they need to do next.
guidelines.why: Users get confused when the app state changes without feedback — they don't know if it's their turn, if something loaded, or if an error occurred.
examples: Show 'Your turn' indicator in games
Show loading states during async operations

guidelines.rule: Do NOT use placeholder implementations. Every function must have real, working logic. Never write empty function bodies, TODO comments, or stub code.
guidelines.why: LLMs under token pressure often produce skeleton code with placeholder functions that compile but do nothing.
examples: call() must actually deduct chips and advance the game
sort() must actually reorder the data

guidelines.rule: When iterating on existing code, preserve ALL working features. Never remove or break functionality that was working in the previous version.
guidelines.why: Feature regression is the #1 user complaint during iteration. New features often accidentally remove existing ones.
examples: If v1 had working card rendering, v2 must still render cards the same way or better

guidelines.rule: Interactive controls (buttons, inputs, sliders) must NEVER overlap with or be hidden behind other elements. Use proper layout with margins and padding so all controls are fully visible and clickable.
guidelines.why: Generated apps frequently have buttons positioned under cards, overlapping with other elements, or pushed off-screen. Users can't interact with controls they can't see or click.
examples: Fold/Call/Raise buttons must be in their own row BELOW the player's cards, not overlapping them
Use flexbox or grid with proper gap spacing

guidelines.rule: Use the CSS/SVG assets provided in the asset pack prompts. If a renderCard() function, card back SVG, felt texture CSS, or chip styles are provided, USE THEM. Do not reinvent simpler versions.
guidelines.why: Asset packs provide tested, polished visuals. LLMs often ignore injected assets and create inferior replacements.
examples: Use renderCard('A', '♠') for card faces, not plain text
Use the .chip CSS classes for chip displays

guidelines.rule: When a framework scaffold is injected, copy its `swift-tools-version` comment and `platforms: [...]` line VERBATIM. Do NOT upgrade `// swift-tools-version:5.9` to `6.0`. Do NOT upgrade `.macOS(.v13)` to `.v14`, `.v15`, or `.v16`. Same rule for Node `engines`, Rust `edition`, Android `compileSdk` — use the scaffold's value.
guidelines.why: Toolchain/platform upgrades activate features (Swift 6 strict concurrency, newer SDK APIs) that break scaffold code written for the pinned target. Scaffolds are tested at their pinned targets — changing them is guaranteed to break. Observed 2026-04-14: LLMs repeatedly upgraded `.macOS(.v13)` to `.v15`, which triggered Swift 6 concurrency errors (`'self' used in property access before stored properties initialized`, `'shared' not concurrency-safe`, `onKeyPress` API mismatch) — all of which are not errors at the scaffold's pinned target.
examples: If scaffold first line is `// swift-tools-version:5.9`, your output's first line must be the same — not `// swift-tools-version: 6.0`
If scaffold has `platforms: [.macOS(.v13)]`, keep `.v13` — do not upgrade to `.v14`, `.v15`, `.v16`

guidelines.rule: When a framework scaffold is injected ('## Framework Scaffold:' section), the scaffold's package manifest (Package.swift / package.json / Cargo.toml / build.gradle) is CANONICAL. Count the dependencies and copy ALL of them with EXACT URLs, version strings, and platform target. Do not drop packages. Do not bump versions. Do not change platform.
guidelines.why: LLMs under pressure do two bad things: (1) invent non-existent versions (AudioKit 5.10.0 / 5.12.0 when latest is 5.7.2) — causing 'no versions match the requirement' errors; (2) drop 'less obvious' packages while keeping the one whose name matches the framework (keeping AudioKit but dropping AudioKitUI + SoundpipeAudioKit) — causing 'cannot find type X in scope' errors because those types live in the dropped packages. Both fail the build and waste auto-fix cycles.
examples: If the scaffold has 3 .package() entries, your output must have 3 .package() entries — do not consolidate, do not omit, do not substitute
If the scaffold has 3 .product() on the target dependencies, your output must have 3 .product() — they are all imported by scaffold code

guidelines.rule: When a published web mini-app needs to fetch data from an external HTTPS API (weather, geocoding, Wikipedia, currency rates, jokes, photos, Pokémon, etc.), it MUST route the call through the mChatAI proxy: `https://mchatai.com/proxy/fetch?url=<URL-encoded target>`. Direct cross-origin fetch() calls from a published site are blocked by Content-Security-Policy and will silently fail in production.
guidelines.why: Published mini-apps run under a strict CSP (`connect-src 'self' https://mchatai.com ...`). Direct calls to api.weather.gov, en.wikipedia.org, etc. work in the AIHarness preview but fail once published, which produces 'Could not retrieve data' errors users can't diagnose. The proxy keeps CSP locked down (no data exfil to attacker servers) while letting the curated allowlist of safe public APIs reach published code. The allowlist lives at https://github.com/LawrenceNorman/mchatai-source/blob/main/proxy/allowlist.json — adding a host is a git PR.
examples: BAD: const r = await fetch('https://api.weather.gov/points/47.6,-122.3') — blocked by CSP in production, looks fine in preview
GOOD: const proxied = 'https://mchatai.com/proxy/fetch?url=' + encodeURIComponent('https://api.weather.gov/points/47.6,-122.3'); const r = await fetch(proxied)

guidelines.rule: For ANY game with a human player: there MUST be at least one computer/AI opponent. Never make the user play both sides. The AI opponent does not need to be sophisticated — even random legal moves are acceptable — but it MUST exist so the user has someone to play against.
guidelines.why: Users who ask for a 'playable game' expect an opponent. A game where you control both sides is a sandbox, not a game. This is the #2 most common complaint after auto-pilot.
examples: Card game: AI opponents make decisions automatically after human acts
Board game: computer picks a legal move after human's turn

guidelines.rule: NEVER use library stubs, shims, or abbreviated implementations. If the app needs a chess engine, game physics, or any complex logic — write it inline. Do NOT include a 'minified' library that is actually a stub returning empty values. Do NOT write comments like 'for brevity', 'abridged', 'for the sake of token limits', 'include full X in production'. Every line of code must be real, working logic.
guidelines.why: Models under token pressure produce fake 'minified' libraries that are actually stubs — e.g., a chess.js stub where every method returns null. This produces an app that looks correct on first glance but is completely non-functional. The Evaluator will flag any stub as an automatic failure.
examples: BAD: Chess() constructor where .moves() returns [] and .board() returns []
BAD: Comment saying 'for the sake of token limits this is a stub'

guidelines.rule: When using localStorage for game state (bankroll, score, progress), ALWAYS validate on load. If the saved value is missing, zero, negative, or corrupted, reset to the default starting value. Never trust localStorage blindly — it may have stale data from a previous session, be zero from a game-over state, or be absent on first load.
guidelines.why: A Blackjack game that reads bankroll from localStorage showed $0 on first load because the key didn't exist yet and parseInt returned NaN which displayed as 0. The game was unplayable despite having zero console errors.
examples: GOOD: let bankroll = parseInt(localStorage.getItem('bj_bankroll')) || 100;
GOOD: if (!bankroll || bankroll <= 0) bankroll = DEFAULT_BANKROLL;

guidelines.rule: NEVER use clip-path, overflow:hidden, or mask on a container that holds interactive game pieces (checkers, cards, tokens, characters). The child elements will be clipped by the parent's shape and become partially hidden or unclickable. If you need a shaped background (like a triangle), use a ::before or ::after pseudo-element for the shape, leave the parent as a plain rectangle, and place the game pieces inside the parent with z-index higher than the pseudo-element.
guidelines.why: Backgammon mini-app used clip-path:polygon(...) on the .triangle parent holding the checker children. The checkers got clipped by the triangle shape — their round tops poked out above the triangle line and the bottoms were hidden, and they were unclickable outside the triangle bounds. Same problem would occur for chess squares with corner badges, hex boards with tokens, etc.
examples: GOOD: .triangle { position:relative; } .triangle::before { content:''; position:absolute; inset:0; clip-path:polygon(50% 0,100% 100%,0 100%); background:#c9a176; z-index:0; } .triangle > .checker { position:relative; z-index:1; }
BAD: .triangle { clip-path:polygon(50% 0,100% 100%,0 100%); } .triangle > .checker { ... } — checkers clipped by triangle

guidelines.rule: Interactive game pieces (checkers, cards, chips, tokens) must have z-index HIGHER than their background decoration. Set position:relative on pieces so z-index takes effect. The stacking order from bottom to top should be: board background (z-index 0), board decorations / triangles / grid lines (z-index 1), pieces (z-index 2), selected / highlighted state (z-index 3), dragged piece (z-index 10).
guidelines.why: Game pieces rendered beneath board decorations are unselectable because clicks hit the decoration, not the piece. Even when visible they look wrong because they appear 'behind' the board.
examples: GOOD: .piece { position:relative; z-index:2; } .piece.selected { z-index:3; } .piece.dragging { z-index:10; }
GOOD: Use a fixed stacking context by setting position:relative on the board container itself

guidelines.rule: For pieces that STACK vertically (checkers on a backgammon point, poker chips, cards in a pile): use position:absolute with computed top values, or use flexbox with negative margins (e.g. margin-top:-18px for 30px pieces = 40% overlap). Never stack them flat on top of each other — the user must be able to see the count visually.
guidelines.why: Backgammon points can hold 5+ checkers which need to stack. Without proper stacking CSS they all render at the same position (looks like 1 piece), or they fall outside the point (looks broken).
examples: GOOD (flex): .stack { display:flex; flex-direction:column-reverse; align-items:center; } .stack > .checker { margin-bottom:-18px; } .stack > .checker:first-child { margin-bottom:0; }
GOOD (absolute): pieces[i].style.top = (i * 20) + 'px'; parent is position:relative

guidelines.rule: When there is an Asset Pack or Reference Build that matches the user's request, PREFER to copy its CSS/JS exactly rather than re-inventing. Pack-provided components (like .bg-board, renderDie(), Leaderboard class) are tested and correct. Re-inventing them from scratch wastes tokens and usually produces lower-quality output.
guidelines.why: The Component Library exists so the wizard can reuse known-good code. Ignoring provided assets and rolling your own is the #1 cause of visual/layout bugs that the pack would have prevented.
examples: GOOD: `.bg-board { ... }` copied verbatim from Component Library
GOOD: `container.innerHTML = renderDiceTray([3,5]);` using provided helper

guidelines.rule: Use explicit numeric dimensions for any game container that renders pieces at specific positions. Do NOT use `flex:1` or `1fr` units as the only size constraint — they collapse to 0 when the parent doesn't have a defined size, making the entire board invisible.
guidelines.why: Backgammon board disappeared because `.board { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(13,1fr); }` had no explicit width/height, and its parent chain used min-height instead of height. The grid collapsed to 0x0 and nothing rendered.
examples: GOOD: .board { width:800px; height:550px; aspect-ratio:13/9; } or with explicit min-width/min-height on children
BAD: .board { flex:1; display:grid; } inside a min-height container

guidelines.rule: For any app that has a SCORE (games, quizzes, challenges, typing tests): call `window.mChatAI.leaderboard.submit({ score: finalScore, metadata: { ... } })` when the game/round ends. Then fetch and display the top 10 scores with `window.mChatAI.leaderboard.getTop(10)` on the game-over screen. If `window.mChatAI.leaderboard` is undefined, skip silently (local play mode). Show the user their rank and percentile from the submit response.
guidelines.why: Leaderboard is a zero-config feature of the mChatAI platform. Any scoring app that omits it misses competitive replay value and cross-user engagement. The bridge is always present in deployed apps — skipping the guard `if (window.mChatAI?.leaderboard)` causes errors in local preview mode.
examples: On game over: if (window.mChatAI?.leaderboard) { window.mChatAI.leaderboard.submit({ score, metadata: { level, moves } }).then(r => showRankBadge(r.rank, r.percentile)); }
Fetch top scores: window.mChatAI.leaderboard.getTop(10).then(scores => renderLeaderboardTable(scores));

guidelines.rule: For apps that use mChatAI Cloud AI features (LLM hints, AI opponents, image generation, TTS narration, quiz generation): gate these features behind `window.mChatAI.cloud.isAvailable()`. If cloud is available, show the feature with a compute-token cost badge (e.g., '⚡ 2 tokens'). If unavailable (offline or no account), show the feature as disabled with a 'Requires mChatAI+ account' tooltip. Never show a broken or erroring UI for users without cloud access.
guidelines.why: Cloud-powered features add high value (AI opponent strength, TTS narration, hint generation) but require compute tokens and a connected mChatAI account. Apps that fail silently or crash when cloud is absent frustrate users who are in local/offline mode.
examples: AI Hint button: if (window.mChatAI?.cloud?.isAvailable()) { btn.textContent = 'AI Hint ⚡ 1 token'; btn.onclick = () => window.mChatAI.cloud.aiHint(gameState); } else { btn.textContent = 'AI Hint'; btn.disabled = true; btn.title = 'Requires mChatAI+ account'; }
TTS narration: window.mChatAI.cloud.tts({ text: 'Round 3 begins!', voice: 'nova' }).then(url => new Audio(url).play());

guidelines.rule:
NEVER write literal ` ` (backslash+n, two characters) into JavaScript string literals that become visible text. Either use a real newline character (press Enter in the source), or use `
` tags in HTML, or use CSS `white-space: pre-line`. The JS `' '` escape is fine INSIDE a JavaScript string — it compiles to a real newline. But if you write `.textContent = 'Line 1\n\nLine 2'` with a DOUBLE backslash, the two literal characters ` ` render on the page as visible text, not a line break. Same goes for template literals: `` `Line 1${' '}Line 2` `` is fine; `` `Line 1\nLine 2` `` is wrong.
guidelines.why:
Seen repeatedly 2026-04-22: top-down adventure, vector-shooter arcade, and Hearts sessions all shipped with literal `


` visible on title/overlay screens instead of line breaks. Root cause: generator string-concatenated with double-backslash `\n` thinking it was an escape, but in source code the double-backslash emits two literal characters. Users reported apps as 'blank' or 'showing raw text' — actually functional games with a cosmetic rendering bug.
examples: BAD: `.textContent = 'Score: 0\\n\\nLives: 3'` — page shows literally 'Score: 0\n\nLives: 3'

GOOD: `.innerHTML = 'Score: 0

Lives: 3'` — real line breaks rendered

guidelines.rule: Only use a scaffold file (e.g., `card-game-scaffold.html`, `space-invaders-scaffold.html`, `snake-scaffold.html`, `breakout-scaffold.html`) if the user's Goal EXPLICITLY matches the scaffold's genre. An 'top-down adventure' goal is NOT a card game. A 'Tower Defense' goal is NOT a brick-breaker. A 'Word Puzzle' goal is NOT an arcade shooter. When the goal doesn't match any listed scaffold, build the artifact from scratch using only the universal + visual-quality wisdom rules. Reading a scaffold 'to see the structure' when the genres don't match is a direct path to shipping the wrong game entirely.
guidelines.why: Seen 3x across 2026-04-22/23: user requested top-down adventure but the generator read `card-game-scaffold.html` (listed in cli-project-context.md under 'Card games') and shipped Hearts code under an title. The evaluator then rubric-scored the output as 'a card game' because the CLAUDE.md scaffold reference was still in-context. Root cause: scaffold catalog is a reference CATALOG but CLI agents treat file-path mentions as read-me-now signals. Rule forces explicit genre match before any scaffold read.
examples: GOAL: 'top-down adventure with castles and dragons' → ✓ no scaffold matches → build from scratch, no scaffold reads.
GOAL: 'Hearts card game' → ✓ card-game-scaffold.html is correct; extend it.

guidelines.rule: When evaluating a generated artifact (frontend-taste rubric, visual eval, or auto-grade), judge the artifact's ACTUAL CODE + RENDERED OUTPUT — never the surrounding CLAUDE.md, wisdom-rule examples, or scaffold references in context. The CLAUDE.md tells the GENERATOR where to find resources; it is NOT a description of what was built. If the rubric asks 'CARDS_JUSTIFIED?' and the artifact is a canvas-based dragon-slaying game with NO card components, the answer is N/A (not 'yes because it's a card game' — that would be evaluating the scaffold reference, not the artifact).
guidelines.why: Seen 2026-04-23: evaluator on an top-down adventure artifact reported 'CARDS_JUSTIFIED: 2/2 — The application is a card game' when the actual index.html was a dragon/castle/chalice game with zero card components. Root cause: evaluator read the card-game scaffold path in CLAUDE.md and concluded the artifact was a card game. Without this rule, every non-card-game session gets mis-graded by proximity to scaffold references.
examples: GOOD: evaluator reads generated HTML, finds `<canvas>` rendering dragons + castles + rooms → concludes 'adventure game', rates CARDS_JUSTIFIED as N/A.
BAD: evaluator reads CLAUDE.md section on 'Card games', concludes 'this is a card game', rates CARDS_JUSTIFIED 2/2 based on the scaffold reference instead of the actual HTML.

guidelines.rule: If `visual-brief/tokens.json` exists in the working directory, you MUST read it and use those exact hex values + typography + spacing for the artifact's visual design. Do NOT invent your own palette. Map: tokens.colors.primary → primary CTA / hero color; tokens.colors.accent → highlights, hover, badges; tokens.colors.background → page/canvas background; tokens.colors.surface → cards, panels; tokens.colors.text + textMuted → body text. Use tokens.typography.fontFamily verbatim in CSS `font-family`. Use tokens.spacing.baseUnit (in px) as the base of all paddings/margins (multiples of it). Use tokens.radii.{sm,md,lg} for `border-radius`. Honor `tokens.componentHints` qualitatively (e.g. `glass-panels-with-blur` → `backdrop-filter: blur(...)`, `glowing-CTAs` → CSS `box-shadow` glow on accent). Also read `visual-brief/meta.json` for `referenceStyle` (e.g. `cinematic-dark`) — that's the user's chosen archetype name; align the overall mood to it.
guidelines.why: Phase VD.7.12 ships a Swatch Catalog: the user picks one of 5 pre-baked design swatches BEFORE generation begins, and the picked tokens are written to `visual-brief/tokens.json` in the workDir for the CLI generator to read. Without this rule, the LLM ignores the file and invents its own palette — defeating the entire pre-generation visual picker. Hit 2026-04-26 on a Cavern Descent run: user picked Cinematic Dark (purple #7C3AED + orange #FB923C + near-black bg + glass panels), tokens.json was staged correctly, but Claude CLI never read it and shipped a generic green-and-blue palette.
examples: GOOD: tokens.json says primary=#7C3AED → CSS `:root { --primary: #7C3AED; }`, hero buttons use `var(--primary)`, hover state uses tokens.colors.accent.

GOOD: meta.json says referenceStyle=cinematic-dark + tokens.componentHints includes glass-panels-with-blur → cards get `background: rgba(28,28,32,0.6); backdrop-filter: blur(12px); border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08)`.

guidelines.rule: Mini-apps MUST be designed mobile-first. The header is the worst offender for stealing screen space — total header height ≤ 32px on mobile / ≤ 44px on desktop. For mini-apps using web-components, mount the `ui/MiniHeader` component (path: `ui/MiniHeader.js`, export: `MiniHeader`) — title-only by default; subtitle is OPTIONAL and hides on mobile. Never roll your own hero block. For non-Lego artifacts, the title `<h1>` font-size must be in `clamp(0.95rem, 3.4vw, 1.2rem)` (≈15-19px), no eyebrow line, no brief paragraph in the header. The header ANNOUNCES; the game/tool is the primary content. HUD widgets (score cards, lives, timers, action buttons) belong BELOW the header in their own row — they are NOT part of the header and must also be compact: ≤ 50px row height on mobile, ≤ 1.1rem (~18px) for the value text, ≤ 8px padding. Reserve at least 75% of the viewport height for the actual game/tool content.
guidelines.why: Seen 2026-05-02 across recent generations (Chess, Adventure, Tower Defense, Pyramid Hopper, Mini Crossword): hero blocks with `h1 { font-size: clamp(3rem, 12vw, 7rem); }` plus eyebrow + brief paragraph consumed 200-300px before the game appeared. Then below the header, oversized HUD cards (TURN/MOVES/CAPTURED at ~80px each with `font-size: 2rem` and `padding: 24px`) ate another 100-150px. User feedback: 'still pretty massive ... I'd like to default to a mobile friendly view ... we would still have a whole chessboard that would need to fit below.' Mobile-first means designing for a 360px wide / 640px tall viewport FIRST and scaling up — not the reverse.
examples: GOOD (Lego): import { MiniHeader } from '../../ui/MiniHeader.js'; MiniHeader.mount('[data-mini-header]', { title: 'Chess' }); — title-only, ~28px tall on mobile.

GOOD (non-Lego):

Tower Defense

.

guidelines.rule: Never write internal architecture jargon into user-visible content. Banned phrases in titles, subtitles, eyebrows, instructions, puzzle clues, dialogue, copy: 'Lego', 'Lego blocks', 'web-component(s)', 'mini-app', 'assembled from blocks', 'recipe', 'golden assembly', 'scaffold', 'harness'. These are internal developer terms — they must stay in code comments and JSON markers, NOT in DOM text the player sees. The user sees a polished GAME or TOOL named what THEY asked for; they should never see how it was built.
guidelines.why: Seen 2026-05-02: Mini Crossword shipped with clue text mentioning 'Lego' and 'mini-app' as if they were trivia (e.g. clue 'What Lego blocks help produce'); Chess/Adventure/Tower-Defense ship with eyebrow text 'Chess clone assembled from Lego blocks' visible above the title. Users complained 'the focus on Lego and mini-apps is just bizarre.' Root cause: the example HTML files in mchatai-source/frameworks/web-components/examples/*/index.html include the eyebrow string verbatim, and the LLM copies it into generated artifacts. Wisdom rule + marker check + scrubbed examples close the loop.
examples: BAD eyebrow: 'Chess clone assembled from Lego blocks' — exposes our component model to the player.
BAD crossword clue: 'Container holding mini-app components (8 letters)' — meta-content, not real trivia.

guidelines.rule: When inlining a selected web-components source file (single-file-inline mode), copy the EXACT verbatim file body. Do not abbreviate, summarize, drop methods, omit class fields, or 'simplify' implementations. Only the top-level `import { ... } from '...';` statements may be removed (since they would not resolve inline). Multi-line imports must be removed in their entirety, including the `}` on the last line — do not leave orphaned `} from '...';` fragments.
guidelines.why: Validation 2026-05-03 found two recurring transcription bugs in tower-defense generation: (1) multi-line `import { drawTurretBasic, ... } from '...';` had its `import {` line stripped but the body and `} from '...';` end were kept, producing `Unexpected token '}'` parse errors at runtime; (2) Entity.js and Turret.js were silently abbreviated mid-class-body, with constructor fields and method conditions missing — the canonical-source check caught the divergence but the marker still claimed `ok` for the marker-only check. Both bugs render the entire generated app non-functional.
checklist: Strip the ENTIRE import statement (multi-line or single-line) — never leave a dangling `} from '...';`.
Copy class bodies verbatim. Do not condense `if (a || b || c || d)` chains, do not drop `this.foo = options.foo;` assignments, do not skip helper methods.
If you find yourself shortening anything, stop — emit the exact source bytes between BEGIN/END markers.

guidelines.rule: When the user's goal includes a theme adjective like 'cinematic dark', 'deep purple', 'neon retro', 'sunset', map it to the closest matching swatch in `WEB_COMPONENT_SWATCHES` rather than defaulting to `retro-neon`. Pick by `surface`/`accent` color match, not by id similarity. The current swatches are `retro-neon` (cyan + dark navy), `vector-noir` (pure black + amber), and `sunset-arcade` (deep purple `#2f1734` + warm pink/orange).
guidelines.why: Validation 2026-05-03: `Make me a chess web mini-app with a deep purple cinematic theme` produced `applySwatchVariables(getSwatchByID('retro-neon'))` despite `sunset-arcade` having a literal deep purple `#2f1734` surface. The LLM picked the default swatch and authored an inline custom palette rather than using the matching catalog swatch, defeating the swatch-system contract.

guidelines.rule: Every game with a terminal phase (game-over / you-win / level-complete / mission-failed / bunker-overrun) MUST display a visible on-screen restart affordance. For Lego web-component artifacts, mount `ui/RestartOverlay` (path: `ui/RestartOverlay.js`, export: `RestartOverlay`) and call `overlay.show({ title, subtitle, buttonLabel, onRestart })` when the terminal phase is reached. For non-Lego artifacts, render a centered modal with a button ≥ 60px tall and ≥ 200px wide, labeled clearly ('Restart' / 'Play Again' / 'New Game' — NOT 'OK' or unlabeled), that fully resets game state when tapped. Keyboard support (Enter/Space) is required. Menu-bar shortcuts (Cmd+N, Ctrl+N) are a power-user nicety, NOT a substitute for the on-screen button.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on thrust-and-land: 'There is no restart button for the game: That should be a fundamental wisdom rule to all games if we don't have that already.' Same pattern hit shipped paddle-bounce, burger-stomp arcade, flap-and-joust, Tower Defense — they have menu shortcuts but lack a tappable on-screen affordance. This is the single most common 'great game but UX feels rough' complaint. Marker check `requiresRestartButton` enforces presence of the component import OR an inline restart-element with text matching `restart|play again|new game` that becomes visible during a terminal phase.
examples: GOOD (Lego): import { RestartOverlay } from '../../ui/RestartOverlay.js'; const restart = new RestartOverlay({ host: '[data-app]', onRestart: () => game.reset() }); /* on game-over */ restart.show({ title: 'Game Over', subtitle: `Score: ${score}`, buttonLabel: 'Play Again' });
GOOD (non-Lego): a centered <div> overlay with backdrop blur and a single <button onclick='restart()'>Play Again</button> that's ≥60×200px, focusable, Enter-bindable.

guidelines.rule: Use the canonical --mchat-* token vocabulary from resources/Swatches.js for all themable values: colors (background, surface, text, accent, accentWarm, stroke, shadow), radius (sm, md, lg, pill), spacing (1..6 = 4/8/12/16/24/32px scale), typography sizes (xs..xl, fluid via clamp), and font families (ui, display, mono). NEVER define your own :root { --my-bg, --app-accent, --piano-warm, ... } namespace — invented per-app token names route around the swatch system, so a swatch swap won't re-skin them. If you need a synchronous-paint default (FOUC fallback before applySwatchVariables runs), copy the exact :root block emitted by buildSwatchDefaultsCSS('<swatch-id>') verbatim. Domain-art colors (game piece colors, board cell colors, atmospheric backdrop tints) STAY hardcoded — those are not theme.
guidelines.why: Phase TOK (2026-05-05) extended the Swatches.js token vocabulary from 7 colors to 25 tokens (colors + radius + space + type + font). Audit of ~50 LLM-generated mini-apps found ~90% reinvented their own :root vars (--mm-bg, --cw-accent, --piano-warm) instead of consuming --mchat-*, which routes around the swatch system's reskin promise. Adoption requires the LLM to know the canonical names — this rule lists them. Marker check tests/check_component_usage.mjs reports `rogueRootVars` for any :root declaration outside the canonical vocabulary; will become fail-strict in a follow-up once all framework examples migrate.
examples: GOOD: top of CSS file: `:root { /* mchat-swatch-defaults — synchronous FOUC fallback */ --mchat-background: #020617; --mchat-accent: #22d3ee; --mchat-radius-md: 12px; --mchat-space-3: 12px; --mchat-font-display: 'Avenir Next', 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif; /* …all 25 canonical tokens… */ }` — then consume them: `.card { padding: var(--mchat-space-3); border-radius: var(--mchat-radius-md); background: var(--mchat-surface); }`

GOOD: keep `.chess-square { background: #f8e7c8 }` (cream) and `.chess-square.dark { background: #7a4b2d }` (saddle) hardcoded — those are domain-art colors, not theme. Same for piece colors, atmospheric backdrop gradient endpoints, and game-specific symbol colors.

guidelines.rule: The Web Components catalog index in the prompt lists every component's id, summary, exports, and path. Component SOURCE is NOT pre-loaded in the prompt — fetch it on demand before importing. Two ways: (1) call the `mchatai-web-components` MCP `read_component(<id>)` if available; (2) read the file at `./web-components/<path>` directly from your work dir. Do NOT invent the component's API from its name alone — the catalog index gives you exports, but verifying signatures against the actual source prevents hallucinated APIs (e.g. inventing `gameManager.initialize()` when only `gameManager.start()` exists). Once verified, declare the component in the marker block and import it normally.
guidelines.why: Phase LF.1 (2026-05-05) trimmed the web-components context layer from ~50KB to ~5KB by emitting only the catalog index instead of full source. The save unlocks ~30-50KB of headroom for planner spec, recent history, and reference assets on complex multi-component apps. The risk is that the LLM hallucinates APIs from component names alone; this rule mandates source verification. Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini all run with cwd = the artifact's work dir, so direct file Read works for all three; the MCP path is preferred when installed because it returns CONTEXT.md alongside source.
examples: GOOD: catalog says `entities.chess-rules` exports `ChessRules`. LLM reads `./web-components/entities/ChessRules.js`, sees `setupStandardBoard(board)`, `legalMoves(board, row, col)`, `applyMove(board, move)`. Imports and uses those exact signatures.
GOOD (with MCP): tool-call `mchatai-web-components.read_component('entities.chess-rules')` → returns source + CONTEXT.md → LLM uses verified signatures.

guidelines.rule: Every HUD pill / always-visible counter MUST answer a question the player is actively asking right now. If a value is constant, near-zero, or only meaningful at game-over, it does NOT belong in the always-on HUD. Specifically: (a) BEST/HIGH-SCORE — game-over panel only, never in-play (see score-show-personal-best-on-game-over). (b) COMBO when current value is 1 (i.e. no combo active) — drop it; surface only DURING a chain via a transient `Combo x3!` flash, then hide. (c) CLEARED-counter or kills-counter when value is 0 — drop until the first event. (d) DIFFICULTY label that doesn't change — move to a settings sheet. (e) Tutorial helper text after the player has made N moves — fade out. The gold reference is a 3-pill HUD: SCORE | LEVEL+TARGET+PROGRESS+STARS | MOVES. Anything more is noise; anything less may starve the player of feedback. The acid test: for each pill, ask 'in the next 5 seconds will this number change in a way that affects my decision?' — if no, remove it.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-06 on shipped candy-match: 'how did candy crush regress so much from this really amazing version? ... Seems like we need to shrink the header generally, it looks like it's just trying to fill out 4 big boxes at the top of the screen: What is "Combo X1" even mean?' The shipped version had SCORE | BEST 240 | MOVES | COMBO X1 | CLEARED 0 — five pills, three of them showing zero / one / a stale persistent value. The deployed gold (mchatai.com/hub/candy-match) showed only SCORE + LEVEL/Target/Progress/Stars + MOVES and felt much cleaner. Not a candy-match-specific bug — generalizes to any scored game.
examples: RIGHT (compact 3-pill): [Score 1,200] [Level 3 — Target 5,000 with progress bar + ★★☆] [Moves 22] — answers: how am I doing? what am I aiming at? how much time left?
RIGHT (combo as transient): on a 3+ chain, briefly fly `Combo x3!` text from the board center; never as a persistent pill that says `Combo X1`.

guidelines.rule: When generating a mini-app inside a CLI session (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI), do NOT run `open <file>.html`, `python -m http.server`, `npx serve`, `xdg-open`, or any other shell command that launches the artifact in the user's browser, terminal, or any external app. The host (mChatAI+) installs and previews the artifact in its own webview. A CLI-launched browser tab opens a STALE pre-install copy from the wizard tmp dir (e.g. `~/Library/Containers/com.sevenhillsstudio.mChatAImac/Data/tmp/wizard-<id>/chess.html`), which (a) confuses the user into debugging the wrong file, and (b) misses the recipe-aware install (the tmp file is the bare CLI fallback, not the recipe.<game> assembly that ships). End your turn after writing/replacing the artifact files in the work dir; report what you produced in text. The host takes it from there.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05 chess session: after the wizard's API-only path failed and fell back to claude-cli, the CLI opened `/tmp/wizard-3e1f567a-give-me-a/chess.html` in Safari. That file was the bare-HTML CLI fallback (no recipe, no swatch, hardcoded #1a1a2e palette) — totally separate from the recipe.chess assembly that the host then installed and previewed. User saw two chess games at once, thought one was 'broken' and the other 'rogue,' couldn't tell which to debug. Same pattern hits any CLI-fallback session that emits an HTML file. Universal rule because Codex and Gemini CLIs share the same temptation.
examples: RIGHT (CLI agent ends turn): write index.html + sidecar files to cwd; respond with 'I've written index.html (8 components, recipe.chess marker). The host will install and preview it.'
WRONG: write chess.html, then run `open chess.html` — opens stale tmp file in Safari, user sees a different palette and inconsistent layout from what the host previews.

guidelines.rule: All games default to single-player vs CPU/AI unless the user explicitly says 'two-player', 'multiplayer', 'split-screen', '2P', 'PvP', or names a specific opponent. The CPU/AI plays the opposing role with reasonable competence (beatable but not trivial). For games without a natural opponent (Snake, falling-block puzzle, 2048, Breakout solo-mode), the CPU rule doesn't apply — but for any duel/match-up game (paddle-bounce, four-in-a-row, Tic-Tac-Toe, Checkers, Chess, Poker, Blackjack, Battleship, Air Hockey, Tennis, fighting games, racing games), single-player-vs-CPU is the canonical default. NEVER ship a game that requires two humans on one keyboard/screen by default.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. End user playing solo can't enjoy a forced-2-player game — the other paddle/piece/hand just sits idle. Default 2P makes the app feel broken. PvP is opt-in via UI toggle, not the default. Generalizes a paddle-bounce-specific incident to all duel/match games.
examples: RIGHT: paddle-bounce loads with 'Player vs CPU'. CPU paddle tracks ball with slight delay. Toggle in controls: 'Switch to 2 Players'.
RIGHT: Tic-Tac-Toe loads with 'You vs CPU'. CPU plays minimax or rule-based moves. Toggle: '2 Player Mode'.

guidelines.rule: When `recipe.with-leaderboard` is selected as an ADDON on top of a base game recipe, you MUST: (1) import `GameOverWithRank` and `Leaderboard` using NAMED-IMPORT syntax with curly braces — `import { GameOverWithRank } from "./web-components/ui/GameOverWithRank.js";` and `import { Leaderboard } from "./web-components/ui/Leaderboard.js";`. NEVER `import GameOverWithRank from ...` (default-import) — that resolves to `undefined` at runtime and the game-over screen throws TypeError; (2) on game-over (and ONLY on game-over), call `await GameOverWithRank.show({ host, score: finalScore, metadata, onRestart: () => game.restart() })` — this single call submits the score, renders the rank card OR a 'Sign in at mchatai.com to post your score' CTA when the player isn't signed in, renders the top-10 list, and mounts the Play Again button; (3) DO NOT wrap that call in try/catch with a 'Leaderboard Unavailable' fallback panel — the component is no-op-safe and a fallback adds dead code that swallows real bugs; (4) include both `ui.leaderboard` and `ui.game-over-with-rank` in the marker `components` array; (5) include `recipe.with-leaderboard` in the marker `addons` array, alongside the base recipe id (e.g. `{recipe: 'recipe.snake', addons: ['recipe.with-leaderboard'], components: [...]}`); (6) PREFER `mode: 'single-file-inline'` in the marker and inline the component source bodies with `BEGIN mChatAI Web Component: <id>` / `END` fence markers — `module-imports` only works when the host page bridge is present or relative paths resolve, which breaks at file:// inside the mChatAI+ preview WKWebView (game-over panel never renders). Single-file inline runs identically at file:// and /u/{slug}/. NEVER call `Leaderboard.submitFinal` mid-game. NEVER manually fetch `/api/leaderboards/*` or any HTTP endpoint — always go through the components. NEVER suppress the sign-in CTA when submitFinal returns null — the leaderboard is intentionally the persuasive funnel for free mChatAI account signup; that CTA is the funnel.
guidelines.why: Phase 1 of the hosted high-score service (spec write-up-an-excellent-witty-sparrow.md). The feature is a one-click version-bump applied to existing scoring games via continueDevelopment + a FeedbackTicket whose prose names this recipe. Without this rule, the LLM tends to (a) submit scores during play instead of game-over (violates u-018 + score-show-personal-best-on-game-over), (b) hand-roll a sign-in dialog instead of using the GameOverWithRank-built CTA (loses funnel coherence + each game ships its own auth flow), (c) call fetch('/api/leaderboards/...') directly instead of going through the bridge component (breaks rate-limiting + auth + offline fallback), (d) drop the addons marker field so the gate can't enforce required-component subset, OR (e) use default-import syntax for components that are named-exported, which silently turns the import into `undefined` and forces a try/catch fallback that masks the bug. Hit (e) on the very first canary 2026-05-09 — flappy-leaderboard generation passed the gate but would have crashed in browser. Signed-in is by design — the leaderboard is the reason users sign up at mchatai.com, and the same auth surface unlocks newsletter signup + orders later for non-game mini-apps.
examples: GOOD imports (named, curly braces): `import { GameOverWithRank } from "./web-components/ui/GameOverWithRank.js"; import { Leaderboard } from "./web-components/ui/Leaderboard.js";`

GOOD: on game-over: `await GameOverWithRank.show({ host: document.querySelector('#game-over'), score: state.score, metadata: { level, moves }, onRestart: () => game.restart() });` — no try/catch wrapper, no fallback panel.

guidelines.rule: Post-deploy enhancement offers are data, not code. Every new offer lives in `mchatai-source/harness/enhancement-offers/index.json` with this shape: `{id, displayName, pitch, iconSymbol?, recipeID, appliesToContentCategories[], deterministicMatcher?, llmAppliesWhenPrompt?, feedbackPromptTemplate, alreadyAppliedMarker, keywords?}`. The Swift-side matcher does TWO passes: (1) deterministic — when `deterministicMatcher` names a registered Swift classifier (today: 'scoringGame' → ScoringGameClassifier); (2) LLM — for every offer with `llmAppliesWhenPrompt`, one structured-JSON call (`{matched:[{id,rationale}]}`) classifies them all at once. Output IDs MUST be a subset of input offer IDs — hallucinated IDs are dropped silently. On any LLM failure the matcher returns deterministic-only results so the UI never goes dark.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-12 alongside the spec write-up-an-excellent-witty-sparrow.md as the framework that generalizes the original hand-coded 'Add High Score' banner. Hand-coded bundling each new offer into Swift cost an App Store cycle per offer; this data-driven registry ships new offers via a single mchatai-source PR. Companion files: `enhancement-offers/prompts/<offer-id>.md` for FeedbackTicket prose; `enhancement-offers/judge-prompt.md` for the LLM system prompt. Strict subset-validation prevents the judge from inventing offer IDs that don't exist downstream.
examples: GOOD (deterministic offer): {"id": "offer.add-leaderboard", "recipeID": "recipe.with-leaderboard", "deterministicMatcher": "scoringGame", "llmAppliesWhenPrompt": null, "alreadyAppliedMarker": {"addonsContains": "recipe.with-leaderboard"}, "feedbackPromptTemplate": "prompts/add-leaderboard.md"}

GOOD (LLM-judged offer): {"id": "offer.add-sound-effects", "recipeID": "recipe.with-sound-effects", "deterministicMatcher": null, "llmAppliesWhenPrompt": "Would this game benefit from SFX? Reply yes ONLY when it's interactive AND currently silent.", "alreadyAppliedMarker": {"componentsContains": "resources.audio-manager"}}

guidelines.rule: For any artifact that needs colors (mini-apps, dashboards, charts, themed UI), PREFER the platform's color-palette fundamentals over hand-picked hex values. The autoload catalog lists which palettes the running session can reach (see `mchatai-fundamentals` context layer). Three v1 palettes ship today: (a) `semantic` — success/warning/error/info/neutral × light/base/dark tiers; pick this for status badges, toasts, form validation, anything that communicates state. (b) `tailwind-default` — 22 hue families × 11 tiers (242 colors); pick this for general UI when you need multiple distinct hues for backgrounds/accents/text/charts. (c) `css-named` — 50 W3C-named colors for legacy compatibility or when the artifact needs the literal name (e.g. `red`, `teal`); rarely the right choice for new designs. Selection rule of thumb: 1-state UI → use `tailwind-default` slate/gray/zinc for neutrals + one hue family for accents. Status UI → `semantic`. Don't invent palette hex values when one of these covers your need.
guidelines.why: LLMs default to ad-hoc hex codes that drift across an artifact (one button uses #2563EB, another uses #3B82F6, a third uses #2A6FE0 — visually similar, all 'blue', but the inconsistency reads as AI-generated to users). The platform palettes are designer-curated, WCAG-aware, and reused across artifacts so a user's installed mini-apps share a coherent visual language. Hand-picked hex also forfeits the runtime API: with `window.mchatai.colorPalette(id)`, the user can later re-skin every mini-app that uses `semantic` by editing one source file. Hardcoded hex can't do that.
examples: Semantic status pill (HTML/CSS): const palette = window.mchatai.colorPalette('semantic'); el.style.background = palette.role('error'); el.style.color = palette.role('error', 'dark');
Tailwind chart series (HTML/CSS): const t = window.mchatai.colorPalette('tailwind-default'); chart.colors = ['blue-500', 'emerald-500', 'amber-500', 'rose-500'].map(n => t.hex(n));

guidelines.rule: In mini-apps, use the runtime API `window.mchatai.colorPalette(id)` to fetch palette values at page-load instead of inlining the JSON. The shell injection (install-time auto-bundle by `MchataiMiniAppShellGenerator`) detects calls to `window.mchatai.colorPalette(...)` in your HTML and bundles the referenced palette as a sibling `mchatai-shell.js`. The API surface per palette returned: `{ hex(name) → '#RRGGBB' | null, role(roleName, tier='base') → '#RRGGBB' | null, all() → [{name, hex, role?, tier?}] }`. Quote the palette id as a string literal so the install-time scanner can detect the dependency: `window.mchatai.colorPalette('semantic')` (good — scanner picks it up) vs `window.mchatai.colorPalette(paletteId)` (bad — scanner can't infer the id and may bundle all known palettes defensively, wasting bytes).
guidelines.why: Inlining a palette's JSON in the artifact's HTML defeats the fundamental's reuse property — the artifact stops responding to platform-level palette updates and bloats by ~2-20 KB. The shell injection is lazy: if your artifact never calls `window.mchatai.colorPalette`, no shell entry is added for palettes. Reference by string id once, get the bundle automatically, stay coupled to the platform.
examples: Read once, use many times: const sem = window.mchatai.colorPalette('semantic'); document.querySelector('.btn-error').style.background = sem.role('error');
Hover/active states: const tw = window.mchatai.colorPalette('tailwind-default'); btn.style.setProperty('--bg', tw.hex('blue-500')); btn.style.setProperty('--bg-hover', tw.hex('blue-600'));

guidelines.rule:
When an artifact validates English words (spelling bee, hangman, scrabble, anagram, crossword, five-letter-guess puzzle, word search, vocabulary, autocomplete, spell-check, dictionary lookup), USE the platform's dictionary fundamentals. Do not ship hardcoded word arrays — the platform exists specifically to kill that anti-pattern.

REASON about which dictionary fits based on what the game's mechanics need:

• `english-5letter` — 14,855 words, EVERY word is exactly 5 letters. Curated guess set from the   five-letter-guess puzzle community. Pick this when the game's core mechanic requires 5-letter words   (color-coded grid with green/yellow/gray feedback, 6-guess daily puzzles, '5-letter word game',   any five-letter-guess puzzle-shaped game — even if the user never says 'five-letter-guess puzzle' by name).

• `english-5letter-answers` — 2,315 words, all 5 letters. Curated subset of english-5letter   filtered to 'words common enough to be the day's answer' (e.g. CRANE, AUDIO, BRAVE — not   obscure plurals like TROVS). Pick this when you need to choose a deterministic daily answer   in a five-letter-guess puzzle-shaped game. Pair with english-5letter for guess validation.

• `english-words` — 204,096 real English words, length 3-15. Webster's 2nd International (1934,   public domain). Pick this for ANY word game where word length varies or where the player   enters words of length other than exactly 5: spelling bee (4+ letters), hangman (any length),   scrabble/anagram (any length), word search, autocomplete prefix matching, general spell-check.

RULE OF THUMB — pick by the game's word-length constraint, not by the user's keyword choice:
  - Exactly 5 letters per word (any length-locked guessing game)? → english-5letter
  - Any length / variable / unspecified? → english-words
  - Daily-puzzle answer in a 5-letter game? → english-5letter-answers for the answer pool

If the prompt is ambiguous (e.g. just 'word game'), default to english-words — it covers the most use cases and a player typing 'cat' or 'startup' won't get rejected.

Usage:
  const dict = window.mchatai.dictionary('english-5letter');  // or 'english-words'
  if (!dict.contains(guess.toLowerCase())) toast('Not in dictionary');
  const random = dict.random();  // optional, for daily-answer selection
Quote the id as a string literal so the install-time scanner detects which dictionary to bundle.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-13: prior wisdom said 'if user explicitly says five-letter-guess puzzle, pick english-5letter' — too keyword-rigid. A user saying 'build a 5-letter word-guessing game with 6 tries and color feedback' wouldn't trigger the five-letter-guess puzzle path even though it's structurally a five-letter-guess puzzle. Reframed to describe each dict by WHAT IT CONTAINS so the LLM picks by game-shape (length constraints), not by user vocabulary. Anti-pattern this kills: a hardcoded 16-word list rejecting real words, OR english-5letter chosen for a spelling-bee where players type 4/6/7-letter words and get rejected.
examples: five-letter-guess puzzle-shaped game (5-letter grid, color feedback, 6 guesses): const guesses = window.mchatai.dictionary('english-5letter'); const answers = window.mchatai.dictionary('english-5letter-answers'); const today = answers.words()[Math.floor(Date.now()/86400000) % answers.size()];
Spelling bee / hangman / scrabble (any word length): const dict = window.mchatai.dictionary('english-words'); if (!dict.contains(input)) toast('Not in dictionary');

guidelines.rule: CRITICAL: the `<category>/` subdir is MANDATORY in EVERY web-components import. Write `./web-components/entities/GridBoard.js`, NOT `./web-components/GridBoard.js`. A path that omits the category segment (one of entities/, resources/, ui/, core/, effects/) resolves to NOTHING and the install gate REJECTS the build with 'doesn't resolve to any bundled file'. Each component's full `path` in the catalog index already includes its category (e.g. `entities/GridBoard.js`, `resources/AudioManager.js`, `ui/MiniHeader.js`) — copy it VERBATIM after `./web-components/`. When emitting a Lego-composed mini-app, every `import { … } from "./web-components/<category>/<File>.js";` MUST use an INSTALL-RELATIVE path. The shipped HTML lives at `/installed/<id>/index.html` and its `web-components/` subdir is a sibling. Canonical form for ALL Lego imports — including addon components like `GameOverWithRank` and `Leaderboard` — is `./web-components/<category>/<File>.js` where `<category>` ∈ {core, entities, ui, effects, resources}. Do NOT use `../../ui/...` (that's the source-path the wisdom rules show in examples — it would resolve to a sibling dir of the install root that doesn't exist). Do NOT use `/web-components/...` (root-absolute also breaks). The wisdom rule and example assemblies show source-relative paths so a reader can navigate the catalog; the install path is different. ES module fetch failures are SILENT in WKWebView / headless Chrome dump-dom — no console error, just an empty page — so this bug looks identical to a stale build until you trace network requests.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-13: a leaderboard iteration on a working five-letter-guess puzzle emitted `import { GameOverWithRank } from "../../ui/GameOverWithRank.js"` — the path from u-leaderboard-addon-recipe's example block. In the install bundle that resolves to `/installed/ui/GameOverWithRank.js` which doesn't exist; the ES module load aborted silently and the entire <script type="module"> never ran. Grid + keyboard + leaderboard all blank. The install gate's marker + recipe + syntax checks all passed because the artifact is structurally valid — only the runtime path resolution is wrong. Mini-app install gate LF.6c now auto-rewrites these patterns, but the wisdom rule prevents the LLM from producing them in the first place.
examples: GOOD: import { GameOverWithRank } from "./web-components/ui/GameOverWithRank.js";
GOOD: import { GridBoard } from "./web-components/entities/GridBoard.js";

guidelines.rule:
Never embed a literal newline character inside a single- or double-quoted JS string. JavaScript string literals ("..." and '...') cannot span multiple source lines — a raw line-break inside one is a SyntaxError that breaks the ENTIRE <script> module (no code in it runs, nothing renders). When you need a newline in a string value, either escape it ("
") or switch to a backtick template literal (`...`) which permits multi-line content. This applies everywhere — share-text builders, emoji rows, multi-line tooltips, .split() / .join() separators, regex string patterns. If the resulting code reads e.g. `text + "
"` or `split("
")` or `\`row1
row2\``, you are correct.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-13: a generated five-letter-guess puzzle clone shipped a share-text builder with `+ "<actual newline>"` instead of `+ "\n"`. The script-module parse failed at that line; the entire game never bootstrapped (empty grid, empty keyboard, 4 console errors). The mChatAI install gate marked the build OK (component-usage marker valid, 10 canonical Lego imports), the WebView showed only static HTML, and a human had to debug it. Single-character mistake, total artifact failure. This rule is universal because the failure mode is universal — any string with an embedded line in any web miniapp / webSPA / agent / pipeline-JS will silently break the surrounding module.
examples: GOOD: const shareText = `Row 1\nRow 2`;
GOOD: text += scored.map(c => statusEmoji[c.status]).join('') + '\n';

guidelines.rule: When you're using a `examples/<game>/` reference build from web-components (chess, candy-match, blackjack, poker, frogger, pacman, asteroids, etc.), READ AT MOST THREE FILES: `examples/<game>/index.html`, `examples/<game>/main.js`, `examples/<game>/<game>.css`. STOP. Do NOT then open every component source (ChessRules.js, ChessAI.js, MinimaxAI.js, Board8x8.js, MiniHeader.js, RestartOverlay.js, Leaderboard.js, Card.js, Deck.js, PokerHandEvaluator.js, BlackjackRules.js, …) one by one before you write the install. The example folder IS the canonical usage pattern that already shows how to import and call every component. Reading the underlying component sources is over-engineering with no positive return on the 300-second Claude Code budget. One game's example folder is ≤3 reads; the whole component catalog is 30+ reads and routinely hits `maxTurns-reached` without producing an artifact. Trust the example. Write the miniApp.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-14: Chess, match-three puzzle, and Blackjack all HARD-FAILED with `maxTurns-reached events=44/57/…` because the LLM spent the entire 300s budget on Read tool calls reading ChessRules.js, ChessAI.js, MinimaxAI.js, Board8x8.js, MiniHeader.js, RestartOverlay.js, Leaderboard.js, Card.js, Deck.js, PokerHandEvaluator.js, BlackjackRules.js etc. one-by-one before reaching the write phase. maze-chase arcade (1 turn, 213s, 0 errors) and lane-hopper arcade (1 turn, 244s, 0 errors) PASSED with the same recipe-load, same prompt format — difference is they trusted the example folder. Same regression, three games, three blocked installs.
examples: GOOD: 1) Read examples/chess/index.html. 2) Read examples/chess/main.js. 3) Read examples/chess/chess.css. Now I have the patterns. Writing the install index.html.
GOOD: 1) Read examples/candy-match/index.html. 2) Read examples/candy-match/main.js. Now write.

guidelines.rule: When you `import` a utility from `./web-components/core/Physics2D.js` or any other shared module — `randomBetween`, `clamp`, `lerp`, `vec2`, `aabbIntersect`, `intersectsAABB`, `wrap`, etc. — do NOT also declare a local `function randomBetween(...) { ... }` (or `let`/`const`) with the same name in the same module. ES modules treat top-level `import` bindings as `const`; re-declaring with `function` (a hoisted binding) is a SyntaxError that prevents the entire `<script type="module">` from executing. The page renders the static HTML shell only — no game logic runs, canvas stays empty. If you want a customized version, either ALIAS the import (`import { randomBetween as physRand } from …`) or pick a different local name (`randomInRange`, `localRandom`). If you don't need the imported version at all, REMOVE the import; don't keep it AND redeclare.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-14: vector-shooter arcade canary installed cleanly (6 components, 0 build errors) but ran 2 console SyntaxErrors at line 1406: `Cannot declare a function that shadows a let/const/class/function variable 'randomBetween'.` The LLM had `import { randomBetween } from './web-components/core/Physics2D.js'` at the top AND a local `function randomBetween(a, b) { ... }` further down. The module never executed; the canvas stayed empty. This is structurally indistinguishable from a working install except at runtime — every install-gate check (marker present, imports valid, recipe ID matches) passed, but the page was blank.
examples: GOOD: import { randomBetween } from './web-components/core/Physics2D.js'; // then USE randomBetween(0, 100) directly throughout the file — no redeclaration.
GOOD: import { randomBetween as physRand } from './web-components/core/Physics2D.js'; function randomBetween(a, b) { return physRand(a, b) * 1.5; } // aliasing the import frees the local name.

guidelines.rule: When the prompt's ITERATION CONTEXT section is present — i.e. you are refining an already-installed mini-app — output a JSON PATCH, not a new full HTML artifact. For visual-only edits, use the single-patch `cssAppend` shape. For logic/feature edits (add a CPU player, change a constant, add a new Lego piece, wire a new event), use the `patches` array shape: `{"id": "<existing-id>", "patches": [ {"op": "jsAppend", "code": "…"}, {"op": "jsReplace", "find": "…", "replace": "…"}, {"op": "addModule", "path": "web-components/entities/Foo.js", "source": "…"}, {"op": "cssAppend", "code": "…"} ] }`. Patch op semantics: `jsAppend` appends inside the existing `<script type="module">` body before `</script>`. `jsReplace` substitutes exactly one occurrence of `find` with `replace` (0 or 2+ matches reject the patch — include enough context to make `find` unique). `addModule` writes a new file at `<install-dir>/<path>` — you must ALSO emit a `jsReplace` adding the corresponding `import` line in the same patches array. `cssAppend` mirrors the legacy single-patch CSS protocol. DO NOT emit `<html>`/`<head>`/`<body>` tags. DO NOT re-emit the whole artifact. DO NOT change code outside what the user asked. If the request truly requires a wholesale rewrite (e.g. "convert this to a different game type"), respond with `{"id": "<existing-id>", "fullRebuildRequired": true, "reason": "<one-line>"}` and the harness will fall back to regeneration.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-14 (HKI-001): Chess V1 (2-player) shipped working; user asked 'make black a CPU player'; wizard re-planned, re-selected the recipe, declared `entities/MinimaxAI.js` in the marker, generator emitted a full HTML artifact without importing MinimaxAI → install-gate rejected → V1 was wiped, no V2 installed, user ended with nothing playable. Iterations should patch V1 in place; full re-emission carries two failure modes (gate rejection wipes V1; the regenerated spec drops features V1 had).
examples: GOOD (logic edit): User said 'add a Play Again button'. Emit: {"id":"…","patches":[ {"op":"jsAppend","code":"function setupPlayAgain(){ const btn=document.createElement('button'); btn.textContent='Play Again'; btn.onclick=()=>location.reload(); document.body.appendChild(btn); } setupPlayAgain();"}, {"op":"cssAppend","code":"button{padding:10px 20px;font-size:1rem;}"} ]}

GOOD (add CPU): User said 'make black a CPU'. Emit: {"id":"…","patches":[ {"op":"addModule","path":"web-components/entities/MinimaxAI.js","source":"export class MinimaxAI { pickMove(state){ /* …full body… */ } }"}, {"op":"jsReplace","find":"import { Board8x8 } from \"./web-components/entities/Board8x8.js\";","replace":"import { Board8x8 } from \"./web-components/entities/Board8x8.js\";\nimport { MinimaxAI } from \"./web-components/entities/MinimaxAI.js\";"}, {"op":"jsAppend","code":"const ai = new MinimaxAI(); /* …turn handler hook… */"} ]}

guidelines.rule: Lego rules-module methods like `rules.applyMove(board, move, color)`, `rules.move(...)`, `rules.tryMove(...)` MUTATE the board array in place and return a BOOLEAN indicating whether the move was legal. They DO NOT return a new board. NEVER write `board = rules.applyMove(board, …)` — that assigns `true`/`false` to your board variable, and every subsequent move silently no-ops because `board[index]` becomes undefined. ALWAYS treat the return as a validity check: `if (!rules.applyMove(board, index, currentPlayer)) return;` — bail on illegal moves, then continue with the (now-mutated-in-place) board. This applies to TicTacToeRules, ChessRules, CheckersRules, ConnectFourRules, and any other Lego rules-module with `applyMove`-shaped methods. The canonical example at `examples/<game>/main.js` or `examples/<game>/<GameName>.js` shows the correct pattern.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-14: tic-tac-toe shipped with `board = rules.applyMove(board, index, currentPlayer);` at the click handler. First click made `board` equal `true`; every subsequent click hit `if (gameOver || board[index]) return;` where `board[index]` was undefined → click no-op'd. Game was unplayable but install-gate passed (marker valid, imports resolved). The module's own docstring says 'applyMove(board, move) mutates in place' AND the canonical example at examples/tic-tac-toe/TicTacToeGame.js:95 uses `if (!this.rules.applyMove(this.board, index, HUMAN)) return;` — LLM had the right pattern available, ignored it.
examples: GOOD: if (!rules.applyMove(board, index, currentPlayer)) return; // bail on illegal move; board is now mutated in place with the legal one.
GOOD: const ok = rules.applyMove(board, move, AI); if (!ok) { console.warn('AI picked illegal move'); return; } render(board);

guidelines.rule: When the install-gate's parse-check rejects your previous artifact with `Duplicate top-level function declaration: function <NAME>(...) appears twice at module scope`, repair by DELETING the second declaration. ES module strict-mode treats top-level function declarations as lexical bindings — declaring `function <NAME>(…){…}` twice at module scope is a SyntaxError that prevents the entire `<script type="module">` from executing (canvas stays blank). The two declarations were almost certainly written by accident (cut/paste glitch); one of them is redundant. Pick exactly ONE of the repairs below — do not invent new ones. (1) If the bodies are identical or near-identical, delete the SECOND copy entirely. (2) If you ALSO imported `<NAME>` from a `./web-components/<…>.js` module, the local declaration is the redundancy — delete the local; the import is the single source of truth. (3) If you specifically wanted a tweaked LOCAL version on top of an import, rename the local to `local<NAME>` or `myLocalName` so the names no longer collide — never rename the imported binding. Do NOT keep both declarations and rely on hoisting; ES modules are strict and reject this. Do NOT respond by emitting a fresh full HTML artifact that re-introduces the same duplicate — the auto-fix turn must shrink the previous output, not rewrite it.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-14 (HKI-004): vector-shooter arcade canary emitted `function randomBetween(min, max){ … }` at lines 897 AND 1402 — bodies were byte-identical. Install-gate's LF.10c pre-check caught the duplicate and kicked back for an auto-fix; the LLM's auto-fix retry produced ANOTHER broken artifact with the same duplicate instead of repairing the specific pattern. The deterministic deletion is structurally safe (identical bodies → same behavior) and unblocks installs immediately; this rule exists so the LLM tier ALSO repairs the pattern correctly when the deterministic patcher's stricter byte-identical guard can't act.
examples: GOOD: Rejection says `Duplicate top-level function declaration: function randomBetween appears twice at module scope`. Search the file for `function randomBetween(`. Two occurrences. Delete the SECOND one in full (signature + body + trailing newline). Keep the first.
GOOD: Rejection names `function clamp`. You also have `import { clamp } from './web-components/core/Physics2D.js'`. Delete the LOCAL `function clamp(…){…}`; rely on the import.

guidelines.rule: For card-game mini-apps (spades, hearts, bridge, euchre, cribbage, poker, anything with a player hand of 5+ cards): the user's own hand MUST be the visual focal point and fit in ONE row at both desktop and mobile widths. Use this proven pattern: (a) the hand container spans full width - escape any parent grid cell via `grid-column: 1 / -1` or position outside the seat-grid; (b) cards do NOT wrap - `flex-wrap: nowrap`; (c) cards overlap aggressively via negative margin so the math works on a 375px-wide phone. Sizing for a 13-card hand: desktop card 100-110px wide x 140-156px tall with margin-right around -72px (effective 36px per card -> 13 cards ~540px total). Mobile card 72-78px x 100-110px with margin-right around -54px (effective 24px per card -> 13 cards ~366px total). On hover, the active card lifts (translateY -12px) and its right margin relaxes (-16 to -24px) so the rank is fully readable before the click. Opponent hands are face-down `card.back` stacks at 28-40px wide tucked next to each seat label - tiny, informational, never dominant. NEVER wrap a 13-card hand into 3-4 narrow rows; the player needs to see every card at a glance.
guidelines.why: Catalog regression 2026-05-15: a Spades v3 canary shipped with user-hand cards at 48x68px constrained inside a 3-column grid cell, wrapping to 4 cards per row on desktop and 3 per row on mobile. User-feedback: 'cards are too small', 'I cannot even see my own cards', 'not releasable'. Even after a card-size CSS patch, the cards still wrapped because the hand was constrained by the parent grid. The release-quality fix is full-width edge-to-edge fanning with aggressive overlap on small screens.
examples: GOOD (spades, 13 cards): .player-hand { width:100%; flex-wrap:nowrap; padding:4px 6px } .seat-south { grid-column:1/-1 } .player-hand .card { width:108px; height:152px; margin-right:-72px } @media (max-width:640px) { .player-hand .card { width:78px; height:110px; margin-right:-54px } }
GOOD (heads-up poker, 2 hole cards): .player-hand { flex-wrap:nowrap; gap:8px } .player-hand .card { width:110px; height:156px; margin-right:0 } - no overlap needed when N<=5; full size each.

guidelines.rule: Reserve the TOP-RIGHT corner of the viewport (approximately 200x60 CSS pixels) as EMPTY space. The mchatai.com serve layer (serveUserProject) auto-injects four chrome pills there after publish: Hub (right:8px), Trophy (right:46px), Feedback (right:84px), Sign-in (right:122px) at top:8px. If the generated artifact places a HUD title, hand counter, phase indicator, score badge, or any other persistent element in that area, it will be overlapped by injected chrome. Do NOT use `position:fixed` or `position:absolute` with `top:0; right:0` for any critical UI. If a HUD spans the full top, use a 3-column grid (`grid-template-columns:1fr auto 1fr`) with content on left + center and an empty right column, OR add `padding-right: 200px` to leave room.
guidelines.why: Every published hub mini-app gets the same top-right pill strip injected at serve time. Artifacts that place their own UI in this corner end up with overlapping chrome that obscures both the artifact and the platform controls. User reported 2026-05-18 after a Hearts build where the Phase indicator landed in the top-right and collided with the (would-be) Trophy pill area.
examples: HUD using `display:flex; justify-content:space-between` with 2 children pushes the second child to the top-right corner — instead use `display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr auto 1fr` to keep content centered with empty right column.
